section.title: Orderly Worship
section.sectionRationale: Having grounded the gifts in the one Spirit, chapter 12, and crowned them with love, chapter 13, Paul now applies both to the disordered worship at Corinth. Their love of tongues had made their gatherings unintelligible and chaotic. Paul's governing rule is edification: pursue love, seek gifts that build up the church, and let everything be done for the good of all, decently and in order, because God is not a God of disorder but of peace.
section.thesis: In the assembly the church should pursue love and desire above all the gifts that build others up, so prophecy, which is understood, is to be preferred to uninterpreted tongues, and everything is to be done for edification, decently and in order, because God is not a God of disorder but of peace.
section.frameNote: Four movements: prophecy builds up the church more than uninterpreted tongues; intelligible speech is what edifies; tongues and prophecy as signs to unbelievers; and order in the assembly, so that all is done for edification, decently and in order.

units.label: Prophecy builds up more than tongues

verses.text: Earnestly pursue love and eagerly desire spiritual gifts, especially the gift of {{propheteia|prophecy}}.
For he who speaks in a {{glossa|tongue}} does not speak to men, but to God. Indeed, no one understands him; he utters mysteries in the Spirit.
But he who prophesies speaks to men for their {{oikodome|edification}}, encouragement, and comfort.
The one who speaks in a tongue edifies himself, but the one who prophesies edifies the church.

I wish that all of you could speak in tongues, but I would rather have you prophesy. He who prophesies is greater than one who speaks in tongues, unless he interprets so that the church may be edified.

units.label: Intelligible speech edifies
units.note: five clear words over ten thousand in a tongue

verses.text: Now, brothers, if I come to you speaking in tongues, how will I benefit you, unless I bring you some revelation or knowledge or prophecy or teaching?
Even in the case of lifeless instruments, such as the flute or harp, how will anyone recognize the tune they are playing unless the notes are distinct?
Again, if the trumpet sounds a muffled call, who will prepare for battle?
So it is with you. Unless you speak intelligible words with your tongue, how will anyone know what you are saying? You will just be speaking into the air.
Assuredly, there are many different languages in the world, yet none of them is without meaning.
If, then, I do not know the meaning of someone’s language, I am a foreigner to the speaker, and he is a foreigner to me.
It is the same with you. Since you are eager to have spiritual gifts, strive to excel in gifts that build up the church.
Therefore, the one who speaks in a tongue should pray that he may interpret.
For if I pray in a tongue, my spirit prays, but my mind is unfruitful.
What then shall I do? I will pray with my spirit, but I will also pray with my mind. I will sing with my spirit, but I will also sing with my mind.
Otherwise, if you speak a blessing in spirit, how can someone who is uninstructed say “Amen” to your thanksgiving, since he does not know what you are saying?
You may be giving thanks well enough, but the other one is not edified.
I thank God that I speak in tongues more than all of you.
But in the church, I would rather speak five coherent words to instruct others than ten thousand words in a tongue.
Brothers, stop thinking like children. In regard to evil be infants, but in your thinking be mature.
It is written in the Law: “By strange tongues and foreign lips I will speak to this people, but even then they will not listen to Me, says the Lord.”
Tongues, then, are a sign, not for believers, but for unbelievers. Prophecy, however, is for believers, not for unbelievers.
So if the whole church comes together and everyone speaks in tongues, and some who are uninstructed or some unbelievers come in, will they not say that you are out of your minds?
But if an unbeliever or uninstructed person comes in while everyone is prophesying, he will be convicted and called to account by all,
and the secrets of his heart will be made known. So he will fall facedown and worship God, proclaiming, “God is truly among you!”

units.label: Order in the assembly
units.note: God is not a God of disorder but of peace

verses.text: What then shall we say, brothers? When you come together, everyone has a psalm or a teaching, a revelation, a tongue, or an interpretation. All of these must be done to build up the church.
If anyone speaks in a tongue, two, or at most three, should speak in turn, and someone must interpret.
But if there is no interpreter, he should remain silent in the church and speak only to himself and God.
Two or three prophets should speak, and the others should weigh carefully what is said.
And if a revelation comes to someone who is seated, the first speaker should stop.
For you can all prophesy in turn so that everyone may be instructed and encouraged.
The spirits of prophets are subject to prophets.
For God is not a God of disorder, but of {{eirene|peace}}, as in all the churches of the saints.
Women are to be silent in the churches. They are not permitted to speak, but must be in submission, as the law says.
If they wish to inquire about something, they are to ask their own husbands at home; for it is dishonorable for a woman to speak in the church.
Did the word of God originate with you? Or are you the only ones it has reached?
If anyone considers himself a prophet or spiritual person, let him acknowledge that what I am writing you is the Lord’s command.
But if anyone ignores this, he himself will be ignored.
So, my brothers, be eager to prophesy, and do not forbid speaking in tongues.
But everything must be done in a proper and orderly manner.

propheteia.gloss: Prophecy, a Spirit-prompted message spoken to people for their edification, encouragement, and comfort. Because it is understood, it builds up the church, convicts outsiders, and instructs all; Paul urges the Corinthians to desire it above uninterpreted tongues, always to be weighed by the others against Scripture.

glossa.gloss: Tongue, meaning both the physical organ and a language. In this chapter it is the gift of speaking in a tongue that no one present understands unless it is interpreted. Paul values it but insists that in the assembly what cannot be understood cannot edify, so tongues need interpretation to serve the church.

oikodome.gloss: Building up, edification, literally the construction of a house. It is the governing rule of the chapter: every gift in the gathering is measured by whether it builds up the church. What edifies the whole body is to be preferred, which is why intelligible prophecy outranks uninterpreted tongues.

eirene.gloss: Peace, wholeness, and well-ordered harmony. God is not a God of disorder but of peace, so worship that reflects His character is orderly rather than chaotic. Peace here is not mere quiet but the settled order that lets the whole church be edified and God be honored.

basic.context: The Corinthians loved the gift of tongues and let it dominate their gatherings, even when no one could understand what was said. Paul does not ban tongues, but he insists that the point of coming together is to build up the church, and that only happens through what people can understand. So he urges them to pursue love and to prefer prophecy, which speaks plainly to people. He then gives practical rules so that worship is orderly, because the God they worship is a God of peace, not confusion.
basic.hermeneutics: This is applied instruction for the gathered church, flowing directly from love, chapter 13, and the one body, chapter 12. The controlling principle, edification, must guide how we read the specific rules. Some directions address a particular disorder at Corinth, but the abiding principles are clear: worship should be intelligible, orderly, and aimed at building up everyone, because that reflects God's own character.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul prefers propheteia, prophecy, because it is understood and so edifies, over a glossa, a tongue, that no one can follow without interpretation. The whole chapter is governed by oikodome, building up: every gift in the gathering is measured by whether it edifies the church. And the reason worship must be orderly is that God is a God of eirene, peace, not disorder.
basic.typology: The strange tongues Paul cites from Isaiah were a sign of judgment on an unbelieving Israel who would not listen. Ordered, Spirit-filled worship in which an outsider falls down and confesses God is truly among you fulfills the promise that the nations would come and worship the God of Israel. The gathered church becomes the place where God's presence is made known.
basic.christ: The Spirit's gifts exist to build up the body of Christ and to make Christ known, so that even an unbeliever, convicted by plain prophecy, falls down and worships God. Christ is the head who orders His body, and the peace that must mark the assembly is the peace He secured and gives. Worship that edifies and is orderly displays the character of the Lord who is its center.
basic.god: God is not a God of disorder but of peace, and this truth governs the whole chapter. What we do in worship should reflect who God is. He desires that all be built up, that outsiders be drawn to worship Him, and that His people gather in a way that mirrors His own peace and order rather than chaos and self-display.
basic.crossReferences: Acts 2 records tongues understood by hearers at Pentecost. Romans 12 urges gifts used in proportion to faith for the body. 1 Corinthians 12 grounds this chapter in the one body and one Spirit. 1 Corinthians 11 shows women praying and prophesying in the assembly, bearing on the instruction in verses 34 and 35.
basic.oneStory: God has always gathered a people to worship Him, and He means their worship to draw the nations in. Corinth's chaos obscured His presence; ordered, understandable, loving worship reveals it, so that outsiders confess God is truly among you. This points toward the great assembly of the redeemed from every tongue who will worship God in perfect order and peace around His throne.

context.literary: The final chapter of the unit on spiritual gifts, chapters 12 to 14, applying the one body and the primacy of love to the assembly. It alternates between comparing tongues and prophecy and giving concrete directions, all under the banner of edification, and closes with a summary command: everything must be done in a proper and orderly manner.
context.historical: Corinthian worship prized ecstatic tongues as the height of spirituality, producing gatherings that could look, to an outsider, like madness. Paul writes to a young church in a status-driven, spectacle-loving city, redirecting them from self-display toward building up others, and toward worship intelligible and orderly enough to convict and welcome the outsider.
context.canonical: This is the New Testament's fullest guide to the conduct of congregational worship, setting edification, intelligibility, and order as governing principles. Its teaching on prophecy being weighed, on order, and on the roles in the assembly is read alongside 1 Corinthians 11, 1 Timothy 2, and the wider New Testament pattern for the gathered church.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To reorder Corinthian worship around love and edification, preferring intelligible prophecy to uninterpreted tongues, and to establish that because God is a God of peace, everything in the assembly should be done to build up the whole church, decently and in order.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The abiding principles, worship should edify, be intelligible, and be orderly because God is peace, are prescriptive. Some specific measures answer a particular disorder at Corinth, so their application calls for wisdom; the debated instruction in verses 34 and 35 must be read alongside 11:5, where women pray and prophesy in the assembly.

debates.question: What does Paul mean that women are to be silent in the churches, verses 34 and 35?

views.label: A specific restriction within the flow of order
views.case: Since 11:5 assumes women praying and prophesying in the assembly, many read the silence as directed at a particular disruption, such as the disorderly weighing or questioning of prophecies, so women were not to interrupt the judging of prophecy but to ask at home, keeping the same order Paul urges of tongues-speakers and prophets.

views.label: A broader ordering of the assembly's authoritative speech
views.case: Others take it as a wider instruction, consistent with 1 Timothy 2, that in the gathered church the authoritative teaching and public judging of prophecy is not for women to lead, while still allowing the praying and prophesying of 11:5, so submission as the law says orders the assembly rather than silencing every voice.

debates.bearing: Both orthodox readings honor the whole of Scripture, hold together with 11:5, and aim at peace and order in worship rather than the silencing or devaluing of women; the difference is over the scope of the restriction. It is also worth noting the textual point that some manuscripts place verses 34 and 35 after verse 40, though the verses are present in the tradition and are handled here as part of the text.

debates.question: What is the gift of tongues in this chapter?

views.label: Real human languages
views.case: The comparison with the many languages of the world, verses 10 and 11, and the pattern of Acts 2 suggest actual languages unknown to the speaker, which is why an interpreter is needed for the church to understand.

views.label: Spirit-prompted utterance needing interpretation
views.case: That the speaker addresses God and utters mysteries no one understands, verse 2, and that his mind is unfruitful, verse 14, suggests a Spirit-given prayer language that must be interpreted to edify others.

debates.bearing: On either view Paul's point holds: a tongue that no one present understands cannot build up the church unless it is interpreted, so in the assembly intelligible prophecy is to be preferred.

study.originalLanguages: Paul's preference is for propheteia, prophecy, because it is understood and therefore builds up, over a glossa, a tongue, that leaves hearers in the dark unless it is interpreted. The word that governs the entire chapter is oikodome, edification, literally the building of a house: every contribution in the gathering is weighed by whether it builds up the church, so five intelligible words outweigh ten thousand in a tongue. And the deep reason for order is theological: God is a God of eirene, peace, not of disorder, so worship that reflects Him is harmonious and edifying rather than chaotic and self-displaying.

typology.type: Strange tongues as a sign of judgment
typology.fulfillment: Paul cites Isaiah, where foreign tongues signaled judgment on an unbelieving Israel who would not listen; in the church, understood prophecy instead convicts and converts the outsider.

The outsider who falls down confessing God is truly among you fulfills the promise that the nations would come and worship the God of Israel, now realized as the Spirit draws people to Christ through the church.

study.christ: The gifts of the Spirit exist to build up the body of Christ and to make Christ known. Ordered, intelligible worship so displays God's presence that even an unbeliever is convicted and falls down to worship, confessing God is truly among you. Christ is the head who orders His body and the peace that is to mark its gatherings, for He is our peace, and the worship that edifies reflects the character of the Lord at its center. Every gift finds its purpose in exalting Him and building His people up in Him.
study.god: The governing truth of the chapter is that God is not a God of disorder but of peace. Worship is to reflect who God is, so it should be orderly, edifying, and welcoming rather than chaotic and self-centered. God desires that the whole church be built up, that outsiders be drawn in to worship Him, and that His people gather in a manner that mirrors His own peace. Here we meet a God whose very character shapes how His people meet with Him.

crossReferences.note: At Pentecost tongues are understood by hearers in their own languages, a sign that bears on the nature of the gift.
The manifestation of the Spirit is given for the common good, the foundation for edification governing chapter 14.
Women pray and prophesy in the assembly, read alongside the instruction of verses 34 and 35.
The source of the quotation in verse 21, strange tongues as a sign to an unbelieving people.
Instruction on order and teaching in the assembly, weighed together with this passage on the roles of men and women.

study.oneStory: God has always gathered a people to worship Him and meant their worship to draw the nations in. Corinth's chaos hid His presence; worship that is loving, intelligible, and orderly reveals it, so that outsiders confess God is truly among you. This anticipates the great assembly of the redeemed from every nation and tongue who will worship God in perfect peace and order around His throne, the goal toward which the whole story runs.

quiz.q: Why does Paul prefer prophecy to uninterpreted tongues in the assembly?

options.text: Because prophecy is understood and so builds up the whole church, while an uninterpreted tongue does not.
options.note: Verses 3 to 5: prophecy edifies the church; a tongue needs interpretation.

options.text: Because tongues are a false gift that should be banned.
options.note: Paul does not forbid tongues; he ranks understandable speech higher for the gathering.

options.text: Because only prophecy comes from the Holy Spirit.
options.note: Both are Spirit-given; the issue is intelligibility and edification.

options.text: Because prophecy is more impressive to onlookers.
options.note: The point is edification and clarity, not spectacle.

quiz.q: What single principle governs how gifts are used when the church gathers?

options.text: Everything must be done to build up the church.
options.note: Verse 26: all of these must be done to build up the church.

options.text: Everyone should use their most dramatic gift freely.
options.note: Free self-expression is exactly what Paul reins in for the sake of edification.

options.text: Only the leaders should exercise any gift.
options.note: Many may contribute; the rule is that it must edify, in order.

options.text: Whatever feels most spiritual in the moment.
options.note: The measure is edification, not private feeling.

quiz.q: How does Paul say an unbeliever may respond to clear prophecy?

options.text: He is convicted, the secrets of his heart exposed, and falls down to worship, saying God is truly among you.
options.note: Verses 24 and 25 describe conviction and worship.

options.text: He is impressed by the display of tongues.
options.note: Verse 23 says tongues alone may make outsiders think you are mad.

options.text: He is left unaffected either way.
options.note: Prophecy convicts and calls him to account; he is deeply affected.

options.text: He is driven away by the strictness of the rules.
options.note: He is drawn to worship, confessing God's presence.

quiz.q: What reason does Paul give for orderly worship?

options.text: God is not a God of disorder but of peace.
options.note: Verse 33 grounds order in the character of God.

options.text: Because disorder is illegal in the city of Corinth.
options.note: The reason is theological, rooted in who God is, not civil law.

options.text: Because the leaders prefer quiet meetings.
options.note: It is grounded in God's character, not leaders' preference.

options.text: Because spiritual gifts are dangerous and should be limited.
options.note: Paul affirms the gifts; order reflects God's peace, not fear of gifts.

quiz.q: How should verses 34 and 35 on women being silent be read responsibly?

options.text: In light of the whole passage and of 11:5, as ordering the assembly for peace, with orthodox interpreters differing on its exact scope.
options.note: It must be held together with 11:5, where women pray and prophesy, and read under the theme of order.

options.text: As a total ban on women ever speaking or ministering at all.
options.note: That collides with 11:5 and the wider New Testament; the scope is debated among the orthodox.

options.text: As an outdated rule with nothing to teach the church.
options.note: It is part of inspired Scripture and addresses order in worship, not to be dismissed.

options.text: As proof that the passage is not really Paul's teaching.
options.note: The verses are in the text; a manuscript note on their placement does not overturn their authority.

questions: Paul measures everything in the gathering by whether it builds others up. When you come to church, are you thinking mainly about your own experience or about edifying the people around you?
He would rather speak five words people understand than ten thousand they cannot. Where might clarity and love for others matter more than impressive-sounding spirituality in your own service?
Prophecy laid bare the secrets of an outsider's heart until he worshiped. How does it change your view of gathered worship to see it as a place where God draws in those who do not yet believe?
God is a God of peace, not disorder, and our worship should reflect His character. What would it look like for your church's gatherings to display God's peace more fully?

This chapter asks us to weigh contested instructions carefully and humbly alongside the rest of Scripture. How can you hold firm convictions on debated matters while still honoring fellow believers who read them differently?

translationNotes.note: Edification translates oikodome, literally the building of a house; it is the governing measure of the chapter, so every gift in the gathering is weighed by whether it builds up the church.

That tongues are a sign for unbelievers draws on Isaiah 28, where foreign tongues signaled judgment on an unbelieving people; the point is not that tongues evangelize but that unintelligible speech leaves outsiders in the dark.

This instruction must be read alongside 11:5, where women pray and prophesy in the assembly; orthodox interpreters differ on its exact scope. Some manuscripts place verses 34 and 35 after verse 40, though the verses are attested in the manuscript tradition.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 Corinthians 14:1-40; any em dashes in the source have been rendered as commas, with wording unchanged.
